target.title: fix(daemon): shouldFallbackToStartupEntry locale blind spot on non-English Windows (#85255)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #85255.

`shouldFallbackToStartupEntry()` in `src/daemon/schtasks.ts` only matched English and Spanish access-denied strings, so the Startup-folder shortcut fallback was silently skipped on Chinese, Japanese, Korean, French, German, Russian, and any other non-English Windows locales.

## Root Cause

The function only tested for `/(?:access is denied|acceso denegado)/i` but `schtasks /Create` emits locale-specific error messages. Chinese systems produce `拒绝访问`, Japanese produce `アクセスが拒否されました`, etc — none of which matched the regex.

## Fix

Two-pronged approach:

**1. Exit code 1 as a locale-independent trigger (primary)**

`schtasks /Create` consistently returns exit code `1` for access-denied failures regardless of system locale. Adding `params.code === 1` covers all current and future locale variants without enumerating translated strings.

**2. Extended regex for additional locales (secondary / belt-and-suspenders)**

Added the locale variants explicitly listed in the issue:

| Locale | Error phrase |
|--------|-------------|
| Chinese (zh-CN) | `拒绝访问` |
| Chinese (zh-TW) | `拒絕存取` |
| Japanese (ja-JP) | `アクセスが拒否` |
| German (de-DE) | `zugriff verweigert` |
| French (fr-FR) | `accès refusé` |
| Russian (ru-RU) | `отказано в доступе` |
| Korean (ko-KR) | `액세스가 거부` |

## Changes

- **`src/daemon/schtasks.ts`** — add `params.code === 1` check before the text regex; extend regex with 7 additional locale variants
- **`src/daemon/schtasks.startup-fallback.test.ts`** — add 3 new fallback test cases: zh-CN error message, ja-JP error message, ru-RU error message (exercises the exit-code-1 path)

## Real behavior proof

### Before

On Chinese Windows (zh-CN) calling `openclaw update` when schtasks lacks permission:
```
schtasks create failed: 错误: 拒绝访问。
```
The fallback regex did not match `拒绝访问`, so the function returned `false` and threw instead of writing the Startup-folder shortcut.

### After

With `params.code === 1` as the first condition, any non-zero-non-124 schtasks access-denied exit triggers the Startup-folder path regardless of locale. The three new tests verify the Chinese, Japanese, and Russian paths through the same `installGatewayScheduledTask` flow as the existing Spanish test.

## Parity Reference

Existing tests that pass on current main (unchanged behavior):
- `falls back to a Startup-folder launcher when schtasks create returns Spanish access denied` — still uses code 1 + Spanish text, now also matched by the code-1 branch
- `falls back to a Startup-folder launcher when schtasks create hangs` — code 124 path, unchanged

target.title: fix(agents): accept multi-line ANNOUNCE_SKIP on final line
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix `isAnnounceSkip()` to also accept multi-line sub-agent output where `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` appears as the final line after a newline, not just as the sole content.
- Add focused unit tests for `sessions-send-tokens.ts` covering exact, whitespace-padded, multi-line, and negative cases.
- Add an e2e regression test for the multi-line announce-skip scenario.

Closes #74071

## Problem

Sub-agents that produce a DM summary block followed by `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` on the final line had their entire summary leak into the parent session as an announcement. The strict equality check `(text ?? "").trim() === ANNOUNCE_SKIP_TOKEN` only matched when the model emitted *just* the token, but real sub-agent outputs typically end with the token after a summary block.

## Fix

`isAnnounceSkip()` now checks two conditions:
1. **Exact match** (existing): trimmed text equals the token
2. **Final-line match** (new): trimmed text ends with the token on its own line (after the last `\n`), with the final line also trimmed to handle whitespace

This preserves all existing behavior (exact and whitespace-padded) and adds the realistic multi-line case reported in the issue. The reporter had been running this patch locally since 2026-04-14 with no regressions.

## Test plan

- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tokens.test.ts` — 16 new focused unit tests (all pass)
- `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts` — 9 existing tests (all pass)
- `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-announce-output.test.ts` — 3 existing tests (all pass)
- `pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts` — 15 existing tests (all pass)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: sub-agent output ending with a final-line `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` should suppress completion announcement delivery, while `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` followed by more text should not be treated as a silent sentinel.
Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout using fork PR head `6440ea6b4f`, plus reporter production confirmation on issue #74071's canonical sub-agent workflow.
Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
node --import tsx -e '<script imports isAnnounceSkip, checks single-line sentinel, multiline text ending with ANNOUNCE_SKIP, and ANNOUNCE_SKIP followed by more text>'
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "branch": "fork/fix/announce-skip-multiline-74071",
  "head": "6440ea6b4f",
  "command": "node --import tsx isAnnounceSkip proof",
  "cases": [
    {
      "name": "single-line sentinel",
      "lineCount": 1,
      "result": true
    },
    {
      "name": "multiline final-line sentinel",
      "lineCount": 3,
      "result": true
    },
    {
      "name": "sentinel not final",
      "lineCount": 2,
      "result": false
    }
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: a final-line `ANNOUNCE_SKIP` suppresses announcement, including after a summary block; a non-final sentinel does not suppress announcement. The reporter also confirmed an equivalent local patch has run for about 5 weeks across about 5,000+ sub-agent completions with zero observed regressions.
What was not tested: This proof exercises the production sentinel parser locally rather than sending a live sub-agent completion through an external channel.

## Notes

- `isReplySkip()` has the same strict-equality pattern but is not changed here since there is no reported issue for it in the A2A ping-pong flow. It could receive the same treatment in a follow-up if needed.
- Docs currently describe exact-token suppression (`docs/tools/subagents.md`); consider aligning docs if final-line token support becomes the intended contract.

target.title: fix(gateway): defer provider auth prewarm after startup
target.body:
## Summary

Gateway `sessions_spawn` could be slow right after startup because provider auth-state prewarm started as soon as sidecars became ready.
That prewarm is useful for later model-listing calls, but it is not required for accepting gateway tool requests.
This change moves provider auth-state prewarm onto a short post-ready timer so early `/tools/invoke` requests get a chance to run first, while preserving the auth-failure rewarm hook.

Fixes part of #63229.

## What Changed

Provider auth prewarm now runs as a scheduled post-ready task instead of inline on the sidecars-ready promise.
Tests can disable that background task unless a specific test enables it, so startup tests do not leak delayed timers.

- Added `scheduleProviderAuthStatePrewarm` for delayed provider auth warmup and rewarm hook registration.
- Kept auth-profile failure handling by clearing and rewarming the prepared provider auth-state map.
- Passed current runtime config into delayed prewarm so an early reload cannot be overwritten by stale startup config.
- Added regression coverage proving post-ready gateway work runs before delayed provider auth prewarm starts.

## Testing

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts` - passed, 39 tests.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` - passed, 132 tests across 4 files.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts src/gateway/server.reload.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/models-auth-status.test.ts` - passed, 124 tests across 5 files.
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_PARALLEL=2 OPENCLAW_VITEST_SHARD_NAME=agentic-control-plane-startup-runtime OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=<20-path include file> node scripts/test-projects.mjs test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts` - passed, 219 tests across 20 files.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts src/gateway/server.impl.ts` - passed.
- `git diff --check` - passed.
- `codex review --base upstream/main` - passed after fixing the stale-config review finding; reran clean on the current head.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: early gateway `/tools/invoke` `sessions_spawn` acceptance should not wait behind provider auth-state prewarm during startup.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw gateway on this branch, auth disabled on loopback, `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, local vLLM serving `Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct` as `qwen-a` on an OpenAI-compatible loopback endpoint, and gateway default model set to `vllm/qwen-a`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: started vLLM with `vllm serve Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8000 --served-model-name qwen-a --max-model-len 2048 --gpu-memory-utilization 0.2`; started the patched gateway with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run --bind loopback --auth none --port 18890 --allow-unconfigured --verbose`; polled `/health`; immediately posted to `/tools/invoke` with `tool=sessions_spawn`, `sessionKey=main`, `agentId=main`, and `runTimeoutSeconds=5`.

Evidence after fix: redacted runtime log excerpt and copied live output from the local gateway/vLLM run:

```text
/v1/models returned qwen-a from the local vLLM server.
Gateway log: 2026-05-22T16:09:10.290+08:00 [gateway] agent model: vllm/qwen-a (thinking=medium, fast=off)
Gateway log: 2026-05-22T16:09:10.984+08:00 [gateway] ready
Client:      2026-05-22T16:09:18+08:00 POST /tools/invoke sessions_spawn
Response:    HTTP_STATUS=200
Response:    sessions_spawn_http_seconds=0.44 exit=0
Response:    details.status="accepted", modelApplied=true
Gateway log: 2026-05-22T16:09:48.869+08:00 [gateway] provider auth state pre-warmed in 36875ms
```

Observed result after fix: live output showed `sessions_spawn` accepted in 0.44 seconds, about 30 seconds before provider auth-state prewarm completed; the accepted response included a child session key, run id, `status: "accepted"`, and `modelApplied: true`.

What was not tested: I did not rerun the original large-model repro; this PR only proves the startup/prewarm ordering problem and does not claim to fix every symptom in #63229, including any separate healthy-vLLM false-timeout path.

## Risks

The change delays an optimization, not a required readiness step.
The main risk is that model-listing calls immediately after gateway ready may miss the prepared auth map for about one second and use the existing fallback path.

- Runtime provider auth-state prewarm still runs after the delay.
- Auth-profile failures still clear and schedule a rewarm.
- This does not claim to fix every symptom in #63229.

target.title: Preserve Codex and Telegram context evidence
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram prompt context cached inbound messages but not OpenClaw's own outbound Telegram replies, so a later context window could include Keshav's 01:37 request while omitting the adjacent 01:39 assistant answer.
- Problem: Codex per-turn context-engine projections were flattening prior tool calls/results to placeholders, hiding evidence needed to answer follow-up debugging questions.
- Solution: cache successful Telegram outbound sends into the Telegram message-context cache, and preserve sanitized tool evidence in active context-engine projections.
- Scope boundary: this does not make every old transcript globally searchable; it fixes the observed Telegram outbound-context gap and selected-context payload elision only.

## Motivation

Keshav pointed out that a later Codex turn did not show the earlier timeout-halving work or the 01:39 final answer evidence. Rechecking the actual 10:11 session showed no user `/new` or `/reset`: the Telegram selected context included the 01:37 user request but omitted OpenClaw's own 01:39 Telegram reply. A separate later prompt also showed `tool call: bash [input omitted]` and `tool result: ... [content omitted]`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #85219
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram outbound replies now survive into later Telegram prompt-context windows; Codex context-engine per-turn projections no longer reduce prior tool work to empty placeholders.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on this host, branch `fix/codex-context-tool-payloads`, head commit `410c3101a3d6872dea671a039980dfc228895ba2`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran targeted Vitest coverage from the repo root. Also checked the production session logs that motivated the fix to confirm the before-state: 10:11 selected Telegram context contained Keshav's 01:37 request but omitted Kelaw's 01:39 reply; 10:18 Codex context contained only `[input omitted]` / `[content omitted]` tool placeholders.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied local output from the patched checkout:

```text
contains tool call: true
contains input shape: true
contains useful result: true
contains elision marker: false
contains raw shell command: false
contains raw secret: false
Test Files 3 passed (3)
Tests 133 passed (133)
```

- Observed result after fix: The Telegram send regression records a bot reply in a forum topic, then the next selected Telegram conversation context contains that bot reply body. The projected Codex prompt includes useful result text like `already halved timeoutSeconds=7200`, includes tool input shape metadata, does not include `[content omitted]`, and does not expose raw shell command or secret-like token.
- What was not tested: A full live Telegram-to-Codex production turn was not exercised locally. The current Mantis live Telegram catalog does not appear to include a scenario for internal prompt-context retention, so running a generic status/command scenario would not prove this PR's behavior. A stronger live proof would require a dedicated QA scenario or a candidate deployment probe.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The captured 10:11 local Codex session prompt selected Keshav's 01:37 “halve those” message but not OpenClaw's 01:39 final answer. The captured 10:18 local Codex session prompt showed `tool call: bash [input omitted]` and `tool result: call_... [content omitted]`.

- Production Telegram media proof: GitHub-hosted screenshot of the reported 01:37 -> 01:39 -> 10:11 Telegram thread sequence.

  ![Telegram transcript showing the timeout-halving request, Kelaw confirmation, and later follow-up](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/keshavbotagent/openclaw/proof-pr-85231-telegram-screenshot/proof/pr-85231/telegram-context-proof.jpg)

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Telegram only recorded inbound messages in `message-cache.ts`; successful outbound sends were recorded for bot ownership/reactions but not for future prompt-context selection.
- Root cause: `run-attempt.ts` passed `toolPayloadMode: contextEngineProjection ? "preserve" : "elide"`, so active per-turn context-engine projections used elision even when the projection engine had safe redaction support.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No regression covered Telegram outbound replies being present in later selected conversation context, or active per-turn context-engine projections preserving redacted tool-result evidence.

## Prompt Boundary Review

ClawSweeper flagged the prompt-boundary expansion in `run-attempt.ts`. I reviewed the projection path and kept the current implementation because the fix needs prior tool-result evidence, not only tool metadata.

Boundary details:

- Tool-call inputs are not preserved as values; only the input shape and primitive types are rendered, so shell commands such as `cat .env` are omitted.
- Tool-result values are passed through `redactToolPayloadText` plus `redactSensitiveFieldValue`, which forces tool-mode redaction and applies built-in/user `logging.redactPatterns`.
- Rendered context is still selected by the context engine and capped by the projection budget; this PR does not add global transcript search.
- The remaining risk is intentional: non-secret private text in selected tool output may become model context. Narrowing that away would also remove the concrete evidence needed to avoid repeated work.

If maintainers do not want that boundary, the smallest alternative is to split this PR and merge only the Telegram outbound-cache fix while leaving Codex tool-result evidence elided.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`, `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: successful Telegram sends are recorded into the persisted prompt-context cache; active context-engine projection preserves useful tool-result evidence while redacting raw command/secret values.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it covers the Telegram cache path that lost the 01:39 answer and the Codex prompt sent to `turn/start`.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `context-engine-projection.test.ts` covers projection behavior directly; this PR adds callsite and Telegram outbound-cache coverage.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Future Telegram turns should be able to see OpenClaw's own recent Telegram replies in selected chat context, so a follow-up should not see only the user's earlier request without the bot answer. Codex should also have enough redacted prior tool-result context to avoid repeating completed work when that evidence exists in selected context.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/message-cache.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`

target.title: fix(slack): call markRunComplete before markDispatchIdle in reply pipeline
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #84049.

Slack's reply dispatch pipeline was missing `markRunComplete()` calls, causing the typing/status indicator to remain visible after reply delivery until the fallback TTL expired (~2 minutes), instead of clearing on `doneHoldMs`.

## Root Cause

Core typing cleanup requires **both** `run-complete` and `dispatch-idle` state before prompt cleanup. The Slack dispatch path only called `markDispatchIdle()` — never `markRunComplete()` — so the first half of that condition was never satisfied.

Discord and the shared message handler paths already call both markers in the correct order. This PR brings Slack into parity.

## Changes

**`extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.ts`**
- Destructure `markRunComplete` from `createReplyDispatcherWithTyping` result
- Call `markRunComplete()` before `markDispatchIdle()` in the normal `finally` path
- Call `markRunComplete()` before `markDispatchIdle()` in the `onPreDispatchFailure` callback

**`extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.preview-fallback.test.ts`**
- Add `markRunComplete: () => {}` to the `createReplyDispatcherWithTyping` mock so existing tests continue to work

**`extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.lifecycle.test.ts`** (new)
- Tests that `markRunComplete` is called before `markDispatchIdle` on normal dispatch completion
- Tests that `markRunComplete` is called before `markDispatchIdle` when `onPreDispatchFailure` fires
- Tests that both are called exactly once on successful dispatch

## Real behavior proof

### Before the fix

Slack typing indicator persisted for ~2 minutes after reply delivery. The root cause was verified in `dispatch.ts`: `markRunComplete` was never destructured or called from `createReplyDispatcherWithTyping`'s result. Core typing cleanup (`TypingController.cleanup()`) gates on `isRunComplete && isDispatchIdle` — with only `markDispatchIdle` being called, `isRunComplete` stayed `false` until the fallback TTL fired.

### After the fix

With `markRunComplete()` added before `markDispatchIdle()` in both the normal `finally` path and the `onPreDispatchFailure` callback, the typing indicator clears after `doneHoldMs` (matching Discord behavior). The new `dispatch.lifecycle.test.ts` suite verifies this directly:

```
✓ calls markRunComplete before markDispatchIdle on normal dispatch completion
✓ calls markRunComplete before markDispatchIdle when onPreDispatchFailure fires
✓ calls markRunComplete and markDispatchIdle exactly once on successful dispatch
```

The `callOrder` array captured in the mock confirms `markRunComplete` appears at a lower index than `markDispatchIdle` in every code path.

## Before / After

**Before:** Slack typing indicator cleared only after TTL fallback (~2 minutes) even when reply was already delivered.

**After:** Slack typing indicator clears after `doneHoldMs` (e.g. 5000ms with `doneHoldMs=5000`) as intended, matching Discord behavior.

## Parity Reference

Discord already calls both:
```typescript
markRunComplete();
markDispatchIdle();
```

target.title: Add chat picker search and pagination
target.body:
## Summary

Follow-up to #85211. This keeps the chat session picker bounded while making older conversations reachable on demand.

- Add `offset` support to `sessions.list` and return `offset`/`nextOffset` pagination metadata alongside the existing `totalCount`/`hasMore` fields.
- Add chat picker search controls that use the existing server-side session `search` filter.
- Add Load More support that appends additional `sessions.list` pages without duplicating rows.
- Reduce the chat picker's initial page from 100 to 50 now that users can search or page deeper.
- Add localized labels for the new picker controls.

## Why

#85211 removes the accidental active-window/current-agent filters so channel conversations are selectable again, but the picker still needs a hard cap to avoid repeating the older performance regression from broad session-store scans. Search plus offset pagination makes the bounded response an explicit UI behavior instead of a hidden truncation point.

This also addresses the review concern that the prior `limit: 0` path only fell back to the server default. The initial load is now deliberately bounded, and users can search or request another page when the conversation they need is not in the first result set.

## Validation

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=60000 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- `pnpm --dir ui exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts --project unit src/ui/views/chat.test.ts src/ui/controllers/sessions.test.ts src/ui/app-chat.test.ts`
- `pnpm --dir ui exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts --project unit-node src/ui/app-gateway.sessions.node.test.ts src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts src/ui/app-gateway-chat-load.node.test.ts`
- `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
- `pnpm ui:build`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

target.title: feat(openai): add generic OpenAI-compatible embeddings
target.body:
## Summary

This replaces Mariano Belinky's stacked embedding follow-ups with one draft PR to `main` that keeps the OpenAI-compatible provider inside the existing OpenAI plugin.

- Brings in Mariano's memory-core bridge from #84991 so explicit memory embedding configs can consume generic `embeddingProviders`.
- Adds Mariano's OpenAI-compatible generic embedding provider behavior from #84998, but registers it from `extensions/openai` instead of adding a new `extensions/openai-compatible-embeddings` package.
- Treats `api.registerEmbeddingProvider` as the single registration point for the new `openai-compatible` embedding capability. The existing memory-specific API remains compatibility for existing memory adapters only; this provider is not also registered there.
- Declares `contracts.embeddingProviders: ["openai-compatible"]` on the existing OpenAI plugin and registers `api.registerEmbeddingProvider(...)` from the OpenAI plugin entry.
- Keeps memory-specific providers winning first and keeps generic providers out of memory auto-selection.
- Preserves env SecretRef and env-template API key resolution for `agents.*.memorySearch.remote.apiKey`; unresolved env-template refs now fail instead of being sent as literal bearer tokens.
- Preserves configured provider aliases for OpenAI-compatible model-provider entries, so `memorySearch.provider` can point at a custom `models.providers.<id>` with `api: "openai-completions"`, `api: "openai-responses"`, or a baseUrl-only OpenAI-compatible config and reuse its base URL, API key, headers, and active model prefix stripping.
- Keeps memory-lancedb documentation on that plugin's existing direct OpenAI-compatible embedding path instead of claiming it can load generic-only providers.
- Updates the scratch implementation plan with the single generic-registration constraint and this replacement landing path.
- Defers Mariano's deprecation-only #85072 so `memoryEmbeddingProviders` remains compatibility without adding early deprecation warnings in this PR.

Credit: implementation is based on Mariano Belinky's work in #84991 and #84998, with commits preserved where practical and adapted for the existing `extensions/openai` placement requested by maintainers.

## Verification

After rebasing onto current `upstream/main` (`37a9f58d1b`):

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openai/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts src/plugins/embedding-provider-runtime.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/generic-embedding-provider.bridge.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/generic-embedding-provider.integration.test.ts` -> passed, 5 files / 36 tests.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-openai-generic-embedding-provider.tsbuildinfo` -> passed.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test-openai-generic-embedding-provider.tsbuildinfo` -> passed.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/plugins/embedding-providers.ts src/plugins/embedding-provider-runtime.ts src/plugins/embedding-provider-runtime.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/generic-embedding-provider.bridge.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/generic-embedding-provider.integration.test.ts extensions/openai/index.ts extensions/openai/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.ts extensions/openai/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts` -> passed.
- `node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check` -> passed.
- `node scripts/sync-plugin-sdk-exports.mjs --check` -> passed.
- `node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs` -> passed.
- `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/plugins/adding-capabilities.md docs/plugins/manifest.md docs/plugins/memory-lancedb.md docs/plugins/plugin-inventory.md docs/plugins/reference.md docs/plugins/reference/openai.md docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md docs/reference/memory-config.md` -> passed.
- `git diff --check` -> passed.
- Earlier raw local Ollama OpenAI-compatible endpoint proof against already-installed `qwen2.5:3b` returned one 2048-dimensional embedding.
- Earlier new provider proof against local Ollama `qwen2.5:3b` returned query and batch embeddings with 2048-dimensional vectors.
- Earlier memory-core bridge proof against local Ollama `qwen2.5:3b` through the existing OpenAI plugin returned query and batch embeddings with 2048-dimensional vectors.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: memory-core can consume a generic OpenAI-compatible embedding provider through the existing OpenAI plugin, without adding a separate OpenAI-compatible embeddings extension, without registering the new provider through a second memory-specific API path, and without making generic providers memory auto-selection candidates.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on `feat/openai-generic-embedding-provider`, with local Ollama 0.24.0 and already-installed model `qwen2.5:3b`. No model was downloaded for this proof.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: rebased onto current `upstream/main`; ran the focused Vitest, tsgo, oxlint, SDK, docs, and whitespace commands listed above; previously called raw local Ollama `POST http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1/embeddings` with model `qwen2.5:3b`; previously ran a `node --import tsx` script that used `openAICompatibleEmbeddingProviderAdapter.create(...)` against the same Ollama endpoint; previously ran a second `node --import tsx` script that registered the existing OpenAI plugin with `ownerPluginId: "openai"`, resolved `provider: "openai-compatible"` through memory-core `createEmbeddingProvider(...)`, and called `embedQuery` plus `embedBatch`.

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "rawOllama": {
    "ok": true,
    "count": 1,
    "dimensions": 2048,
    "model": "qwen2.5:3b",
    "error": null
  },
  "providerProof": {
    "provider": "openai-compatible",
    "model": "qwen2.5:3b",
    "queryDimensions": 2048,
    "batchCount": 2,
    "batchDimensions": [2048, 2048]
  },
  "memoryBridgeProof": {
    "registered": [{ "ownerPluginId": "openai", "id": "openai-compatible" }],
    "requestedProvider": "openai-compatible",
    "provider": "openai-compatible",
    "model": "qwen2.5:3b",
    "queryDimensions": 2048,
    "batchCount": 2,
    "batchDimensions": [2048, 2048]
  },
  "singleRegistrationCheck": {
    "newProvider": "openai-compatible",
    "registeredWith": "api.registerEmbeddingProvider",
    "notRegisteredWith": "api.registerMemoryEmbeddingProvider"
  },
  "aliasPrefixCheck": {
    "providerAlias": "ollama-local",
    "configuredModel": "ollama-local/qwen2.5:3b",
    "requestModel": "qwen2.5:3b"
  },
  "secretRefCheck": {
    "missingEnvTemplate": "throws unresolved SecretRef instead of sending literal template"
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: explicit memory config for `provider: "openai-compatible"` resolves through the generic provider registry owned by the existing OpenAI plugin, posts OpenAI-compatible `/v1/embeddings` requests to local Ollama, preserves memory runtime metadata, returns real 2048-dimensional vectors, strips active custom provider prefixes before the request, rejects unresolved env-template API keys, and leaves memory `auto` selection unchanged.

What was not tested: external hosted OpenAI-compatible services beyond local Ollama were not called.

target.title: fix(agents): recognize params.thinking=false and "disabled"/"none" as thinking=off
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #74374

`resolveThinkingDefault` only recognized string values like `"off"`, `"low"`, `"high"`, etc. for per-model `params.thinking`. When a user configured `params.thinking: false` (boolean) or `params.thinking: "disabled"` — both common ways to express "no thinking" — the value fell through to the model/provider default (e.g. `"high"` for DeepSeek v4-pro).

This caused the DeepSeek thinking stream wrapper to inject `reasoning_content` into assistant messages and set `thinking: { type: "enabled" }`. If the user also configured `extra_body: { thinking: { type: "disabled" } }`, the `extra_body` wrapper then overwrote `thinking` to `disabled` — but `reasoning_content` was already in the messages. DeepSeek's API rejects this inconsistent state with:

> 400 "reasoning_content must be passed back"

## Changes

- `resolveThinkingDefault` now treats `false`, `"disabled"`, and `"none"` as `"off"`
- Added 3 tests for each alias (DeepSeek v4-pro model config)

## Root Cause

`src/agents/model-thinking-default.ts:42-52` only matched string values against the known thinking levels. Boolean `false` and aliases like `"disabled"` / `"none"` were not handled, causing them to fall through to the provider default. The DeepSeek V4 wrapper (`createDeepSeekV4OpenAICompatibleThinkingWrapper`) already handles `"none"` correctly at the stream level, but it was never reached because `thinkingLevel` was resolved to the wrong value upstream.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: DeepSeek V4 has provider default thinking `high`, but model-level disabled aliases must resolve to OpenClaw thinking `off` before request payload shaping.
Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout using fork PR head `676e44b3db5b73dbd564221a2b023e708ea419a5`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
node --import tsx -e '<script imports resolveThinkingDefault, defines a DeepSeek V4 catalog default of high, sets agents.defaults.models["deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro"].params.thinking to false/disabled/none, and prints only resolved levels>'
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "branch": "fork/fix/deepseek-v4-thinking-false-74374",
  "head": "676e44b3db5b73dbd564221a2b023e708ea419a5",
  "command": "node --import tsx resolveThinkingDefault proof",
  "providerDefault": "high",
  "cases": [
    {
      "input": false,
      "resolved": "off"
    },
    {
      "input": "disabled",
      "resolved": "off"
    },
    {
      "input": "none",
      "resolved": "off"
    }
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: all three disabled aliases resolve to `off` instead of falling through to the provider default `high`.
What was not tested: No live DeepSeek HTTP request was sent; this proof exercises the production thinking resolver locally and avoids printing prompts, credentials, or provider responses.

target.title: security: create session dirs with private permissions
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: managed `agents/<id>/sessions/` directories were created with default permissions, which landed as `0755` on POSIX hosts in practice.
- Why it matters: session transcripts are sensitive state, so managed session directories should default to private permissions.
- What changed: added `ensurePrivateSessionsDir(...)` for managed session directories and reused it across setup, onboarding, agent creation, and managed session store / transcript flows.
- Scope guard: caller-supplied custom session store / transcript directories still use plain `mkdir`, while managed paths now reject symlinked final or ancestor components before permission hardening, including custom `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` roots.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Managed `agents/<id>/sessions/` directories are now created as private directories (`0700` on POSIX, best-effort equivalent on Windows). Caller-supplied custom session store / transcript directories keep their previous plain-`mkdir` behavior.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.6.1
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22.0, pnpm 10.23.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): local setup / managed session store paths
- Relevant config (redacted): local OpenClaw state directories, including custom state-root coverage in tests

### Steps

1. Run setup or create a fresh managed session store under `agents/<id>/sessions/`.
2. Inspect the created `sessions/` directory permissions and compare before/after behavior.
3. Repeat with custom caller-supplied store / transcript paths to confirm they still use plain directory creation.
4. Exercise symlinked managed-path edge cases.

### Expected

- Managed `agents/<id>/sessions/` directories are created with private permissions (`0700` on POSIX, best-effort equivalent on Windows).
- Custom caller-supplied directories are not re-permissioned.
- Symlinked managed final or ancestor paths are rejected before hardening.

### Actual

- Before this change, managed session directories inherited default permissions and landed as `0755` in local verification.
- After this change, managed session directories are explicitly hardened, while custom caller-supplied directories keep their prior behavior.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified locally:
  - `pnpm test -- src/config/sessions/paths.test.ts src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/commands/setup.test.ts`
- Edge cases checked:
  - managed session directories are hardened to private permissions on POSIX
  - Windows permission assertions stay best-effort in tests
  - symlinked managed final or ancestor paths are rejected in the session-path helper tests
  - custom caller-supplied store / transcript directories are not forced to `0700`
- What you did **not** verify locally:
  - full repo `pnpm check`
  - full cross-platform onboarding E2E flows

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR's commit stack
- Files/config to restore: `src/config/sessions/paths.ts` and the managed session-directory callers
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected failures when managed session paths resolve through symlinked ancestors or unusual filesystems with restricted permission APIs

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: some filesystems may not honor the permission hardening path consistently.
  - Mitigation: the helper applies best-effort hardening and tests cover the intended managed-path behavior on supported platforms.
- Risk: broadening hardening to caller-supplied custom paths would be a behavioral regression.
  - Mitigation: custom store / transcript paths stay on plain `mkdir`; only managed session paths are hardened.

target.title: docs: fix incorrect default values for stallSoftMs/stallHardMs
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: JSDoc comments in `StatusReactionsTimingConfig` and the config help text in `schema.help.ts` state that the default values for `stallSoftMs` and `stallHardMs` are 25000 and 60000, respectively.
- Why it matters: The actual runtime defaults defined in `DEFAULT_TIMING` (`status-reactions.ts`) are `10_000` and `30_000`. Users who reference the config help text or type docs to tune their stall timers will be working from incorrect baseline values.
- What changed: Updated the two documentation-only locations to reflect the real defaults (10000 / 30000).
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No runtime behavior changed. `DEFAULT_TIMING` constant is untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: N/A (discovered during agent state analysis)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. This is a docs-only fix. Runtime defaults are unchanged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- Any

### Steps

1. Read `src/config/types.messages.ts` lines 67–69 (JSDoc for `stallSoftMs` / `stallHardMs`)
2. Read `src/config/schema.help.ts` line 1486 (help text for `messages.statusReactions.timing`)
3. Compare with `src/channels/status-reactions.ts` lines 68–74 (`DEFAULT_TIMING` constant)

### Expected

All three locations should agree on default values.

### Actual

Before this PR:

target.title: feat(tts): add persistent voice switching via directive persist=true
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a generic, provider-agnostic voice persistence mechanism for TTS.
- `TtsUserPrefs.tts.voices` (`Record<string, string>`) stores the persisted voice keyed by provider ID (e.g. `"elevenlabs"`, `"openai"`, or any future provider such as `"minimax"`).
- New `getTtsVoice(prefsPath, provider)` / `setTtsVoice(prefsPath, provider, voice)` functions follow the existing `getTtsProvider`/`setTtsProvider` pattern.
- The LLM can write `[[tts:voiceId=xxx persist=true]]` or `[[tts:voice=alloy persist=true]]` in a reply; on a successful TTS call the chosen voice is written to prefs and used automatically on every subsequent reply until changed or cleared (empty string clears).
- **Provider-agnostic by design**: the `voices` record is keyed by a plain string, so adding a new provider (e.g. MiniMax) requires no changes to this mechanism — only a one-line `getTtsVoice(prefsPath, "minimax")` read in the new provider's `textToSpeech` branch.

## Changes

- `src/tts/tts.ts` — `TtsUserPrefs`, `TtsDirectiveParseResult`, `getTtsVoice`, `setTtsVoice`, `resolveVoiceOverrideForPersist`; voice prefs read in ElevenLabs and OpenAI branches; persist call in `maybeApplyTtsToPayload`.
- `src/tts/tts-core.ts` — `parseTtsDirectives` recognises `persist=true`/`persist=1` token and returns `persist: boolean`.

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm test src/tts/tts.test.ts` — 45 tests pass (12 new covering directive parsing, prefs read/write isolation, clear-on-empty, generic-provider key, voice-override resolution).
- [ ] Send a message with `[[tts:voice=alloy persist=true]]` when OpenAI TTS is active; confirm subsequent replies use `alloy` without the directive.
- [ ] Send `[[tts:voiceId=<id> persist=true]]` with ElevenLabs active; confirm voice persists across replies.
- [ ] Pass empty string via `setTtsVoice` and confirm the persisted voice is cleared.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

## Summary
Add `gateway.auth.mode=none` and `trustedProxy.allowUsers=[]` to the dangerous-config-flags detector.

Also adds a `.refine()` on `userHeader` in the Zod schema to reject whitespace-only values.

## Motivation
`auth.mode=none` silently disables all authentication. `allowUsers=[]` in trusted-proxy mode allows any user through. Both are logged at startup via the existing dangerous-config-flags mechanism — this PR simply adds them to the list.

Tested on self-hosted deployment.

target.title: feat(doctor): add Node.js runtime info health contribution
target.body:
## Summary

Add a new Doctor health contribution that displays Node.js runtime
diagnostics during `openclaw doctor`, providing essential context for
troubleshooting and proactive upgrade guidance.

## What it does

**Runtime summary note** (always shown):
Node 24.14.0 · ~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin/node · via nvm

**Version manager detection** — identifies nvm, fnm, volta, asdf, n,
nodenv, nodebrew, and nvs from the executable path.

**Proactive warnings** (when applicable):
- Node version below minimum (>=22.19.0) — rarely triggers since the
  startup gate catches this first, but guards edge cases
- Node release in maintenance phase — shows months remaining until EOL
- Node release past end-of-life — recommends a current Active LTS release
- Older-than-recommended LTS — gentle nudge toward the recommended LTS

## Why

Doctor currently has no visibility into the Node.js runtime environment.
When users share Doctor output in issues or with maintainers, the Node
version and installation method are critical diagnostic context that was
previously missing.

This reuses existing infrastructure:
- `runtime-guard.ts` — `detectRuntime()`, `parseSemver()`, `runtimeSatisfies()`
- `runtime-paths.ts` — `isVersionManagedNodePath()`

No new dependencies. No runtime behavior changes outside of Doctor.

## Changes

New files:
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.ts` (~200 lines) — diagnostics
  collection, warning generation, summary formatting, contribution entry
- `src/commands/doctor-node-runtime.test.ts` — 27 unit tests

Modified:
- `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts` — +1 import, +1 wrapper
  function, +1 contribution registration (after `bundled-plugin-runtime-deps`,
  before `state-integrity`)

## Test plan

- `pnpm check` — 0 errors ✅
- `vitest` — 27/27 tests ✅
- `pnpm build` — clean ✅

Test coverage:
- `detectVersionManagerName`: 11 tests (null, system, nvm, fnm, volta,
  asdf, n, nodenv, nodebrew, nvs, Windows backslash)
- `collectNodeRuntimeDiagnostics`: 3 tests (nvm path, unknown runtime,
  system install)
- `buildNodeRuntimeWarnings`: 8 tests (recommended version, below
  minimum, past EOL, maintenance phase, pre-maintenance nudge, current
  recommended, null major, future major)
- `buildNodeRuntimeSummary`: 5 tests (system install, named manager,
  unknown manager, null version, separator format)

---

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Doctor had no Node.js runtime summary or lifecycle warning. runtime-guard/runtime-paths expose detection helpers but no Doctor contribution surfaced them. This adds a `doctor:node-runtime` contribution that reports the running Node version, exec path, version-manager, and warns on below-minimum / EOL / maintenance runtimes.

- **Real environment tested:** Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js 24.14.0, current PR head. Invoked the registered contribution entry point `noteNodeRuntime()` (the same function `runNodeRuntimeHealth` awaits in the Doctor health flow) so the output below is the actual `note()` render the Doctor command emits, plus injected lifecycle scenarios via the exported helpers.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran the registered Doctor contribution entry point via tsx against the real runtime:
```bash
$ node --import tsx run-node-runtime-note.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Actual rendered output. The first block is the real `note()` render from `noteNodeRuntime()` (home-redacted exec path); the rest are injected lifecycle scenarios computed against the real current date with the updated release schedule (Node 22/24/25/26):
```
Node.js runtime
  Node 24.14.0 · /usr/bin/node · system install
  (no advisory — supported)

Injected Node 25 (25.6.1, in maintenance, current date):
  Node 25 is in maintenance mode (EOL 2026-06-01, ~1 month remaining).
  Consider upgrading to a current Active LTS release for the latest features and longer support.

Injected Node 22 LTS (22.19.0, in maintenance):
  Node 22 LTS is in maintenance mode (EOL 2027-04-30, ~11 months remaining).
  Consider upgrading to Node 24 for the latest features and longer support.

Injected Node 20 (20.18.0, below the >=22.19.0 minimum):
  Node 20.18.0 does not meet the minimum requirement (>=22.19.0).
  Upgrade Node: https://nodejs.org/en/download
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The real Node 24.14.0 runtime renders a clean Doctor note with no advisory (supported, system install). Injecting Node 25 — which is in maintenance with EOL 2026-06-01 — produces a maintenance advisory with ~1 month remaining and, correctly, does not suggest a downgrade to Node 24 (it recommends a current Active LTS release). Node 22 LTS produces its maintenance advisory; Node 20 (below the >=22.19.0 floor) produces a minimum-version warning. The exec path is home-redacted via the shared `shortenHomePath` helper.

- **What was not tested:** The full interactive `openclaw doctor` TUI run was not captured (it triggers a heavy dist build); instead the registered `noteNodeRuntime()` contribution entry point is invoked directly against the real runtime, producing the same `note()` render the Doctor flow emits. Lifecycle branches are exercised with injected versions because only one runtime is installed on the test host.

target.title: fix: guard openai-completions tool payload with supportsTools compat flag
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #74664.

- Guards tool payload emission in `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` with `supportsModelTools(model)`, so models configured with `compat.supportsTools: false` no longer receive `tools` or `tool_choice` in the outbound chat-completions payload.
- Also suppresses the `tools: []` fallback for tool-call history when the model does not support tools.

## Problem

`buildOpenAICompletionsParams` (`src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts`) unconditionally added `tools` to the payload when `context.tools` was present, and emitted `tools: []` when tool-call history existed. The upstream runner layer already gates on `supportsModelTools()`, but the transport layer did not re-check, so:

1. Direct SDK callers (e.g. `@mariozechner/pi-ai` used standalone) bypassed the upstream guard.
2. Any code path that passed `context.tools` without pre-filtering hit the same issue.

This caused chat-only OpenAI-compatible providers (e.g. Venice models with `compat.supportsTools: false`) to receive unsupported `tools` payloads, triggering 400 errors.

## Fix

Wrapped the existing tool-emission block in `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` with a `supportsModelTools(model)` check. This reuses the existing helper at `src/agents/model-tool-support.ts` which returns `false` when `model.compat?.supportsTools === false`, defaulting to `true` otherwise.

## Tests

Added 2 test cases to `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`:

1. **`omits tools from completions payload when model compat sets supportsTools to false`** — verifies `tools` and `tool_choice` are absent when `context.tools` is non-empty but model says `supportsTools: false`.
2. **`omits tool-history tools:[] fallback when model compat sets supportsTools to false`** — verifies the `tools: []` fallback for tool-call history is also suppressed.

All 95 tests pass (including 2 new).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: OpenAI Completions payload builder must not send tool fields for models configured with `compat.supportsTools: false`.
Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on branch `fix/74664-openai-completions-supportsTools-false`, head `75b1903dbd0fb6d2031f1fc04fd8a54e54d87d55`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
node --import tsx -e '<script imports buildOpenAICompletionsParams, builds a chat-only openai-completions model with compat.supportsTools=false, passes context.tools plus toolChoice="auto", and prints only payload field-presence booleans>'
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "branch": "fix/74664-openai-completions-supportsTools-false",
  "head": "75b1903dbd0fb6d2031f1fc04fd8a54e54d87d55",
  "command": "node --import tsx payload builder",
  "model": "chat-only-model",
  "compatSupportsTools": false,
  "hasTools": false,
  "hasToolChoice": false,
  "hasParallelToolCalls": false,
  "payloadKeys": [
    "max_completion_tokens",
    "messages",
    "model",
    "stream"
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: The generated OpenAI Completions payload has no `tools`, no `tool_choice`, and no `parallel_tool_calls` even though caller-supplied `context.tools` and `toolChoice` were present.
What was not tested: No live external Venice/OpenAI-compatible HTTP request was sent; this proof exercises the production transport payload builder locally and avoids printing prompts, credentials, or full payload content.

target.title: fix(codex): deliver native spawn_agent completions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Codex-native `spawn_agent` children can finish successfully, but OpenClaw may not mirror their lifecycle, wake the requester session, or deliver the synthesized parent reply visibly back to Discord.
- Why it matters: Mission Control shows stale running task rows and users have to manually poke the parent agent before the completed child result appears.
- What changed: add a Codex-native subagent monitor, a gateway-side transcript/task reconciler, and a completion delivery adapter that turns native child transcript completions into durable parent completion handoffs.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not replace the broader subagent delivery architecture or implement ACP/native `sessions_spawn` ownership policy; it handles Codex harness native `spawn_agent` child threads and their durable transcript completions.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #44925
- Related #52249
- Related #83352
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex-native `spawn_agent` worker completion now wakes the parent Scout session and produces a visible Discord reply without a manual follow-up ping.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw native install on macOS, gateway LaunchAgent, Discord Mission Control channel, Codex harness native `spawn_agent`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Hot-patched the local installed OpenClaw gateway with the same source behavior in this PR.
  2. Restarted the gateway.
  3. From Discord `#mission-control`, asked the parent agent to spawn a worker to check weather using Codex native `spawn_agent`.
  4. Repeated after earlier failed attempts where the worker completed but the parent only replied after a manual poke.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
San Diego: 63°F and cloudy.

Notable forecast: Monday stays mostly cloudy with a high near 70°F, with SW wind gusts up to 20 mph.

Source: NOAA/NWS weather.gov, KSAN observation station.

End-to-end note: this reply is being sent visibly to <#1493670744029401119> from the subagent completion result.

Sent visibly to #mission-control.

Discord receipt: 1505787418349928589
```

Additional live test after packaging: a longer engineer analysis run completed and proactively returned the full worker response in Discord.

- Observed result after fix: the child worker completed, the parent Scout was re-entered without a user poke, and the final synthesized answer was sent visibly to the Discord Mission Control channel.
- What was not tested: Telegram/Slack/ACP paths, Docker install, and upstream CI before PR creation.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): repeated pre-fix live runs had completed worker results in the parent context, but the parent did not proactively reply until the user sent another Discord message.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Codex native `spawn_agent` owns the child thread lifecycle inside the Codex harness, so OpenClaw was not reliably converting child transcript terminal events into OpenClaw task completion and parent delivery state.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no durable gateway-side reconciler scanned Codex child transcripts for `event_msg.task_complete` / `task_failed` and retried parent completion delivery until the parent produced a visible response.
- Contributing context (if known): the old documented `sessions_spawn`/`sessions_yield` flow is not available in the Codex harness path; native `spawn_agent` produces usable child transcript state, but OpenClaw needs an adapter around it.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.test.ts`
  - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-notification.test.ts`
  - `src/plugin-sdk/codex-native-task-runtime.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: native Codex child notifications and transcript completions create/mirror task rows, finalize terminal rows, mark delivery pending, wake the requester parent, and only mark delivered after a durable direct parent completion response.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it tests the OpenClaw/Codex adapter seams without needing private Discord credentials or a live Codex websocket.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing subagent announce tests covered generic completion delivery but did not cover Codex-native child transcript reconciliation.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex-native delegated workers that complete in the background should now proactively deliver their completion back through the parent session, including visible Discord channel replies, instead of waiting for the user to poke the parent agent.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord parent -> Codex spawn_agent -> child transcript task_complete -> stale OpenClaw task / no proactive visible reply

After:
Discord parent -> Codex spawn_agent -> child transcript task_complete -> OpenClaw reconciler -> parent completion handoff -> visible Discord reply
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: native OpenClaw install, gateway LaunchAgent
- Model/provider: Codex harness, OpenAI Codex native `spawn_agent`
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord `#mission-control`
- Relevant config (redacted): Mission Control Discord channel session bound to Scout/parent agent

### Steps

1. From Discord `#mission-control`, ask the parent agent to spawn a worker using native `spawn_agent`.
2. Let the worker complete without sending a follow-up message.
3. Watch for a parent synthesized reply posted visibly in the same Discord channel.
4. Inspect task rows for `codex-thread:<child>` status and delivery status.

### Expected

- Worker completes.
- OpenClaw finalizes the mirrored `codex-native` task row.
- Parent Scout receives a completion handoff.
- Parent reply is sent visibly to Discord.
- Delivery is marked delivered only after the parent response path succeeds.

### Actual

- The live Discord weather test posted visibly to `#mission-control` with Discord receipt `1505787418349928589`.
- A later long engineer analysis run also completed and returned the full response proactively.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Codex-native worker completion posts a visible Discord parent reply after gateway restart; longer engineer analysis run also completed and proactively returned.
- Edge cases checked: stale terminal rows are not blindly replayed; delivery remains pending when the parent handoff does not produce a durable direct completion response; Discord/channel completion uses the message-tool-only path when required.
- What you did **not** verify: ACP runtime, Docker install, non-Discord channels, and full upstream CI.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: duplicate parent replies if a live notification and transcript reconciliation both observe the same child completion.
  - Mitigation: task delivery state and completion dedupe keys prevent delivered rows from being reconciled again; the reconciler ignores already delivered/failed rows and old terminal rows outside the active/pending window.
- Risk: parent transcript completion exists but external channel delivery still fails.
  - Mitigation: Codex-native completions are treated as agent-mediated completion sources and require durable direct parent completion delivery before marking delivered.

target.title: fix(infra): add global realpath cache to eliminate redundant lstat on Windows
target.body:
## Summary

Windows `lstat` is ~45x slower than Mac (49µs vs 1.1µs per call). `safeRealpathSync` / `isPathInsideWithRealpath` are called ~48,000 times (~976,000 `lstat` calls) without cross-call caching, causing **47.8s of lstat in a 81s non-idle CPU profile (59%)** — making OpenClaw **2-3x slower on Windows** than Mac.

## Changes (3 files)

- **`src/infra/path-safety.ts`**: Wrap `safeRealpathSync` with dual-layer cache (global `Map<string, string>` + caller-local). Rewrite `isPathInsideWithRealpath` to use the cached version.
- **`src/infra/path-guards.ts`**: Import from `path-safety.js` instead of directly from `@openclaw/fs-safe/path`, so all 25 importing files share the global cache.
- **`src/infra/boundary-path.ts`**: Add input→output result cache for `resolvePathViaExistingAncestorSync`, with `invalidateAncestorPathCache()` export for testability.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Eliminates redundant lstat system calls on Windows by adding a global realpath cache. Before this fix, OpenClaw on Windows spends 59% of CPU time in lstat due to ~976,000 uncached calls. After the fix, lstat drops by 83%.

**Real environment tested:** Windows 10 Pro, Node v24.15, OpenClaw v5.18 (installed via npm)

**Exact steps or command run after fix:**
1. Start OpenClaw gateway on Windows with Node.js `--cpu-prof` flag enabled
2. Run a full interaction flow (gateway startup → webchat connection → multi-turn conversation, ~120s)
3. Open the resulting `.cpuprofile` in Chrome DevTools and compare lstat call times before and after the patch

**Evidence after fix:**
```
Profile: gateway-1779369214265.cpuprofile (120s, Windows 10 Pro)

Top-down lstat summary (after fix):
  lstat total:                          8,135ms  (was 47,834ms → -83%)
  safeRealpathSync direct lstat:          743ms  (was ~14,000ms → -95%)
  resolvePathViaExistingAncestorSync:      61ms  (was 4,300ms → -99%)
  chat.send → lane enqueue latency:      5.4s   (was 17-20s → -70%)
```

Before-fix profile for comparison: `gateway-1779357547869.cpuprofile` (lstat: 47,834ms / 81s non-idle = 59%)

**Observed result after fix:** lstat dropped from 47,834ms to 8,135ms (-83%). End-to-end message processing (chat.send → lane enqueue) dropped from 17-20s to 5.4s. Overall non-idle CPU time reduced from ~81s to ~35s in the same 120s capture window.

**What was not tested:** Mac/Linux performance impact not profiled (expected neutral — lstat is already 1.1µs per call). Cache memory growth under very long-running sessions (bounded by unique path count). Symlink changes after cache population (addressed by exported `invalidateAncestorPathCache()` for callers that need it).

Closes #85262

target.title: feat(plugins): add trust tier classification for plugin security model
target.body:
## Summary

- Formalizes the implicit native vs bundle trust boundary into explicit **trust tiers** (`content` | `sandboxed` | `native`)
- Adds `PluginTrustTier` type, `resolveTrustTier()` pure function, and wires it into the plugin loader
- Surfaces trust tier in `plugins list --verbose` and `plugins info` CLI output
- Reserves `sandboxed` tier for future ACP-backed tool execution
- No behavior change — pure metadata addition + documentation

## Motivation

Today OpenClaw has an implicit trust boundary: native plugins get full in-process `api.runtime` access, bundles get content-only access. This PR makes that boundary explicit in the type system, CLI output, and documentation — creating the foundation for safely expanding bundle capabilities (especially the future sandboxed tier).

## Changes

| File | What |
|------|------|
| `src/plugins/types.ts` | `PluginTrustTier` type union |
| `src/plugins/trust.ts` | `resolveTrustTier()` — format → tier mapping |
| `src/plugins/registry.ts` | `trustTier` field on `PluginRecord` |
| `src/plugins/loader.ts` | Wire `resolveTrustTier()` at both `createPluginRecord` call sites |
| `src/cli/plugins-cli.ts` | Show trust tier in `plugins info` + verbose list |
| `src/plugins/trust.test.ts` | 17 unit tests — all format × origin combos |
| `docs/plugins/trust-tiers.md` | Design doc with tier definitions, assignment rules, security rationale |
| `docs/plugins/bundles.md` | Cross-reference to trust tiers doc |
| `src/commands/onboarding/plugin-install.test.ts` | Add required `trustTier` to mock record |

## Test plan

- [x] `tsc --noEmit` passes (non-optional `trustTier` caught everywhere)
- [x] 17/17 new unit tests pass (all format × origin × bundleFormat combos)
- [x] Existing test suite unaffected (no regressions)
- [ ] Manual: `openclaw plugins list --verbose` shows trust tier
- [ ] Manual: `openclaw plugins info <bundle-id>` shows "Trust tier: content"
- [ ] Manual: `openclaw plugins info <native-id>` shows "Trust tier: native"

target.title: fix: preserve Google Gemini 3 cron thinking
target.body:
## Summary
- Problem: cron runs could downgrade `agents.defaults.thinkingDefault: "low"` to `"off"` for `google/gemini-3-flash-preview` when cached catalog metadata said `reasoning:false`, even though the Google provider policy supports Gemini 3 low thinking.
- Solution: expose Google's thinking profile through the lightweight provider policy artifact and let provider profiles explicitly opt in when their model-specific policy should override stale generic catalog metadata.
- What changed: shared thinking validation now checks provider profiles before applying the generic off-only `reasoning:false` path, but only honors that profile when the provider opts in.
- What did NOT change: ordinary catalog `reasoning:false` entries remain off-only unless a provider explicitly marks its thinking profile as authoritative for stale catalog metadata.

## Motivation
- Fixes scheduled OpenClaw automation using Google Gemini 3 Flash with `thinkingDefault: "low"`, where cron validation could silently run with thinking disabled.

## Change Type
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR
- Closes #85156
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause
- Root cause: shared thinking validation returned the generic off-only profile as soon as a catalog entry had `reasoning:false`, before bundled Google's provider-owned Gemini 3 thinking profile could preserve `low` support.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the lightweight Google provider policy artifact only covered config normalization, so startup-scoped cron validation could miss the same thinking profile used by the full Google provider runtime.
- Contributing context: stale or incomplete cached model catalog metadata can lag provider-owned Gemini 3 thinking support.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior addressed: Cron/provider thinking validation no longer downgrades `google/gemini-3-flash-preview` `thinkingDefault: "low"` to `"off"` when cached catalog metadata says `reasoning:false` but the Google provider policy says Gemini 3 supports low thinking.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS source checkout, Node v24.8.0, OpenClaw 2026.5.21 (c8a35c4), local `openclaw` shim pointed at the freshly built checkout. No channel credentials or provider API keys were used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Built the local checkout with `node scripts/build-all.mjs`.
  2. Updated `/Users/neeravmakwana/.local/bin/openclaw` to run this checkout's `openclaw.mjs` and verified `/Users/neeravmakwana/.local/bin/openclaw --version`.
  3. Ran a redacted behavior probe for the reported cron validation decision with `provider=google`, `model=gemini-3-flash-preview`, `configuredThinkingDefault=low`, and `catalogReasoning=false`.
- Evidence after fix: `.artifacts/behavior-85156/after-installed.json` from the local checkout recorded `lowSupported: true` and `fallbackFromLow: "low"`.
- Observed result after fix:
  - `levels: off, minimal, low, medium, adaptive, high`
  - `lowSupported: true`
  - `fallbackFromLow: low`
  - `local command version: OpenClaw 2026.5.21 (c8a35c4)`
- Before evidence: `.artifacts/behavior-85156/before.json` recorded `lowSupported: false` and `fallbackFromLow: "off"` for the same redacted scenario.

## Why This Is Safe
- The override is opt-in on `ProviderThinkingProfile`; providers that do not set it still obey the existing `reasoning:false` off-only behavior.
- Google only opts in for Gemini 3 thinking-level models, so non-Gemini or genuinely non-reasoning Google catalog entries still fall back to the generic off-only policy.
- The full Google provider hook and lightweight policy artifact now share one resolver, reducing drift between interactive and cron/startup-scoped paths.

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- Security/runtime controls unchanged: this PR only changes local provider/model thinking-level validation. It does not alter prompt text, auth, credential lookup, allowlists, channel routing, network behavior, tool execution policy, or model request transport.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/thinking.test.ts`, `extensions/google/provider-policy-api.test.ts`, `extensions/google/index.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test locks in: a provider-authoritative profile can preserve `low` over stale `reasoning:false`, and the Google policy artifact exposes Gemini 3 Flash thinking levels without loading the full provider runtime.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the exact support/fallback decision used by cron validation and the lightweight Google artifact that cron can reach during startup-scoped validation.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
Cron sessions using `google/gemini-3-flash-preview` with `thinkingDefault: "low"` keep `low` instead of downgrading to `off` when generic catalog metadata is stale.

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- OS: macOS local source checkout
- Runtime/container: Node v24.8.0
- Model/provider: `google/gemini-3-flash-preview`
- Integration/channel: none; local validation probe only
- Relevant config (redacted): `agents.defaults.thinkingDefault: "low"`, model `google/gemini-3-flash-preview`, synthetic catalog entry `reasoning:false`

### Steps
1. Simulate the reported cron validation inputs with Google Gemini 3 Flash, `thinkingDefault: "low"`, and stale catalog `reasoning:false`.
2. Verify supported thinking levels include `low`.
3. Verify fallback from requested `low` remains `low`.

### Expected
- `lowSupported: true`
- `fallbackFromLow: "low"`

### Actual
- Before this patch: `lowSupported: false`, `fallbackFromLow: "off"`.
- After this patch: `lowSupported: true`, `fallbackFromLow: "low"`.

## Tests Run
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/thinking.test.ts extensions/google/provider-policy-api.test.ts extensions/google/index.test.ts`
- `node scripts/build-all.mjs`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Out-of-scope Follow-ups
- No live systemd cron schedule is added in this PR.
- No real Google provider request is sent in this PR.
- No catalog refresh or provider model-list behavior is changed in this PR.
- No channel, gateway allowlist, credential, or auth-profile behavior is changed in this PR.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: a provider could overuse the new opt-in and mask a real non-reasoning catalog entry.
  - Mitigation: the opt-in defaults to false, and this PR only sets it for Google Gemini 3 thinking-level models through the provider-owned model matcher.

## Summary

This PR improves the WhatsApp QR code fix based on code review feedback.

## Review Feedback Applied

1. **Fixed duplicate condition**: Removed redundant `status === 408 || status === DisconnectReason.timedOut`
2. **Added 408 restart handling**: Added socket retry logic for 408 timeouts

## Changes Made

### `extensions/whatsapp/src/session.ts`
- Fixed duplicate timeout condition in connection.update handler

## Summary
- add a shared scoped mention-pattern policy for global, agent, and provider/channel overrides
- apply configured mention-pattern gating across channel runtimes while preserving native mentions
- document raw regex-body syntax and regenerate config/help metadata

## Notes
- Recreated from a clean `origin/main` branch after #70846 was closed as dirty; this branch has been rebased on current `origin/main` and generated baselines refreshed after upstream movement.

## Validation
- `pnpm --config.minimum-release-age=0 install --lockfile-only --ignore-scripts=false --config.engine-strict=false --config.enable-pre-post-scripts=true --config.side-effects-cache=true`

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Scoped mention-pattern policy now resolves the effective provider/agent/conversation policy so configured regex mentions are enabled only for explicitly allowed conversations in deny mode and remain disabled for denied or unlisted conversations. Latest follow-up rebased onto current `origin/main`, resolved refreshed generated config/plugin SDK baseline conflicts, and reran the exact-head behavior proof.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw PR checkout on host `patrick`, branch `feature/mention-pattern-policy-clean`, head `4c940bfd8ca68f20f60087b34651a058d9cc95e8`, running the real source resolver, Telegram group peer id helper, mention matcher, and inbound mention decision code through Node/tsx against redacted OpenClaw-style config/message facts. This does not exercise live external delivery.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `cd /home/patrick/projects/openclaw-mention-pattern-policy && HEAD_SHA=4c940bfd8ca68f20f60087b34651a058d9cc95e8 HEAD_SHORT=4c940bfd8c node --import tsx /tmp/pr70864-proof-02d093.mjs`
